question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 203km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (72kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (35kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 60km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 92 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 201km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of relief supply box (67kg each) and 4 units of construction material (76kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 77km waypoint, 10 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 89 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 52 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 52min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 78min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 197km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of relief supply box (44kg each) and 8 units of construction material (57kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 22km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 39km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 85 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 63min, 3rd: 78min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 214km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of relief supply box (57kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (22kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 20%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 195km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 85 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 59min, 3rd: 71min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 206km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (76kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (5kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (64kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (8kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 216km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of medical kit (7kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (34kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 20%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 52km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 62min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (58kg each) and 8 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 199km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (80kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 91km waypoint, 1 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 96 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 66min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 194km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (54kg each) and 8 units of construction material (65kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 143km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 88 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 62min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 227km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of construction material (71kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (15kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 144km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 93 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 56min, 3rd: 72min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 214km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (51kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (62kg each). The boat can travel at 44km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 33km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 19%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 26km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 83 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 214km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (65kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (48kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 52km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 87 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 63min, 3rd: 75min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 223km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (64kg each) and 7 units of water barrel (19kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 195km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 98 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 80min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 195km. question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 236km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (80kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (19kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 208km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 215km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 3 units of construction material (63kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 8:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 144km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 85 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 33 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 33min, 2nd: 49min, 3rd: 65min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 211km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of construction material (74kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 20%.
From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 130km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 89 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 57min, 3rd: 73min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 216km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of relief supply box (49kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 91km waypoint, 5 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 97 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 69min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (79kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (59kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 209km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of construction material (68kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 187km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 37 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 37min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 67min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 222km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of relief supply box (65kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (10kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 132km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 93 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 37 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 37min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 69min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 8 units of construction material (59kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
